verses.commentary: Another large crowd, no food. Jesus calls disciples. Similar situation to chapter 6, different location (likely Gentile region).

I have compassion. They have been with me three days with nothing to eat. Jesus notices need. Three days echoes Jonah, resurrection pattern. His compassion is visceral (splagchnizomai, σπλαγχνίζομαι, moved in the inward parts).
If I send them home hungry, they will collapse. Some came from far away. Practical concern. Jesus cares for physical needs. Faith does not exempt from hunger.

Disciples ask: Where can anyone get bread in this remote place? Astonishing question. They witnessed the feeding of 5,000 (chapter 6) but still don't think of Jesus providing. Short memory, hard hearts.
How many loaves? Seven. Different number than before. Jesus works with what they have.
Sit down. He takes seven loaves, gives thanks, breaks, gives to disciples to distribute. Same pattern as before. Eucharistic actions repeated.
A few small fish. He gives thanks for them too. Nothing is too small to bless.
They ate and were satisfied. Seven basketfuls left over. Abundance again. Different type of basket (spuris, σπυρίς, large basket) than in chapter 6 (kophinos, κόφινος, small basket). Details matter.
About four thousand. Smaller than the 5,000, but still massive. After dismissing them, Jesus moves on.
He gets into the boat, goes to Dalmanutha. Location unclear, probably near Magdala. Transition.
Pharisees come, question Jesus, ask for a sign from heaven. They want credentials. Irony: they just missed the feeding miracle. Testing, not seeking.

He sighed deeply. Why does this generation ask for a sign? No sign will be given. The sigh reveals grief. Hardness of heart grieves Jesus. An evil generation seeks signs (Matthew 12:39). Enough evidence exists. More would not convince.
He leaves them, gets back in the boat, crosses to the other side. He withdraws from obstinate unbelief. Judgment by departure.
Disciples forgot bread. Only one loaf with them. Practical problem. Setting for the next lesson.
Watch out for the yeast of the Pharisees and of Herod. Yeast = teaching, influence that spreads. Pharisees = religious hypocrisy. Herod = political compromise. Both corrupt.
They discuss: It is because we have no bread. Literal thinking. They miss the metaphor entirely. Still focused on physical needs.

Jesus aware of their discussion. Why are you talking about having no bread? Do you still not see or understand? Are your hearts hardened? String of rebukes. Harsh diagnosis: hard hearts. Same language used of Pharisees (3:5). The disease is spreading to the disciples.

Do you have eyes but fail to see, ears but fail to hear? Echoes Jeremiah 5:21, Ezekiel 12:2, Isaiah 6:9–10. They are like Israel: sensory organs but no perception. Don't you remember? Memory matters. Past miracles inform present faith.
When I broke five loaves for 5,000, how many baskets? Twelve. They remember the numbers. Cognitive knowledge without spiritual comprehension.
When I broke seven loaves for 4,000, how many baskets? Seven. They recall correctly. Data without understanding.
Do you still not understand? Final rebuke. Evidence is abundant; insight is absent. The question hangs. This is Mark's central problem: the disciples' persistent blindness.
They come to Bethsaida. People bring a blind man, beg Jesus to touch him. Physical blindness about to be healed; spiritual blindness being exposed.
Jesus takes the blind man by the hand, leads him outside the village. Privacy. He spits on his eyes, puts hands on him. Physical contact. Do you see anything? First time a healing is gradual.
I see people; they look like trees walking around. Partial vision. Blurry. Progress, but not completion. The only two-stage healing in the Gospels. Symbolic: the disciples see, but not clearly.
Jesus puts hands on his eyes again. Then he saw everything clearly. Full restoration. Two touches. Gradual healing. Spiritual sight also comes in stages.
Don't even go into the village. Avoid publicity. Messianic secret maintained.
Jesus and disciples go to Caesarea Philippi. Pagan territory, center of Caesar worship. On the way, he asks: Who do people say I am? Public opinion poll. Setting for the great confession.
They reply: John the Baptist, Elijah, one of the prophets. People's guesses are too small. They see prophet, miss Messiah. Right category, wrong identity.
But who do you say I am? Personal question. Peter answers: You are the Messiah (Christos, Χριστός). Correct confession. First explicit acknowledgment from the Twelve. Turning point in Mark.
Jesus warns them not to tell anyone. Messianic secret intensifies. Right confession, but wrong expectations. They must understand what kind of Messiah he is before they can proclaim him.

He begins to teach: the Son of Man must suffer, be rejected by elders, chief priests, teachers of the law, be killed, and after three days rise again. First passion prediction. Must (dei, δεῖ) = divine necessity. Suffering Messiah contradicts all expectations.

He spoke plainly (parrēsia, παρρησία, openly, boldly). Peter takes him aside, rebukes him. Astonishing. Peter just confessed Jesus as Messiah; now he corrects him. He wants a conquering Messiah, not a suffering one.

Jesus turns, looks at disciples, rebukes Peter: Get behind me, Satan! You do not have in mind God's concerns, but merely human concerns. Shocking rebuke. Peter becomes Satan's mouthpiece. The tempter offers an easy path: glory without suffering, crown without cross. Peter thinks human thoughts, not divine ones. This is the great temptation.

Jesus calls the crowd and disciples. Public teaching. Whoever wants to be my disciple must deny themselves, take up their cross, and follow me. Radical call. Self-denial, not self-fulfillment. Cross-bearing, not crown-wearing. Cross = instrument of execution. Follow a crucified Messiah means embrace martyrdom.

Whoever wants to save their life will lose it. Whoever loses their life for me and the gospel will save it. Paradox. Self-preservation leads to loss; self-sacrifice leads to life. Die to live. Lose to gain.
What good is it to gain the whole world yet forfeit your soul? Cost-benefit analysis. The whole world < one soul. No profit in universal acquisition if you lose yourself.
What can anyone give in exchange for their soul? Rhetorical question. Answer: nothing. The soul is priceless, irreplaceable. No trade possible.

Present confession determines future fate. The Son of Man who suffers now will judge later. Shame leads to shame; confession leads to acknowledgment. Chapter eight is the hinge of Mark: from Galilee to Jerusalem, from popularity to passion, from crowds to cross. Peter confesses Jesus as Messiah, then immediately rejects the suffering path. The disciples' blindness is being healed in stages, like the blind man. They see, but not yet clearly.
